title: Exodus 2
subtitle: Birth and Rescue of Moses; Moses Flees; Israel's Groaning

sections.header: Verses 1–10 — The Birth and Rescue of Moses

poi.name: Egypt — Goshen / Nile
poi.text: Moses is hidden for three months, then placed in a basket among the reeds at the bank of the Nile — the river Pharaoh had commanded be used to kill Hebrew boys. The geography of threat becomes the geography of rescue: the Nile kills and the Nile saves.

poi.name: Pharaoh's daughter at the Nile
poi.text: Pharaoh's daughter comes to bathe at the Nile — a routine activity for Egyptian nobility. The river is Egypt's sacred space, the god Hapy's domain. The covenant's future deliverer is drawn out of the same sacred river that had been weaponized against him.

tl.name: Moses born — this passage
tl.text: Moses is born into the decree to kill Hebrew boys. His mother hides him three months, then places him in a basket on the Nile. Pharaoh's daughter draws him out — the name Moses (drawn out) encodes the rescue. The deliverer is shaped by the same decree that threatens Israel.

tl.name: Moses kills Egyptian; flees to Midian (age 40)

notes.note: MacArthur: Moses's mother placed him in a papyrus basket (tevah -- the same word for Noah's ark). As Noah was preserved through judgment-waters, so Moses is preserved through the Nile's death-sentence.
MacArthur: The name Mosheh encodes his entire vocation: rescued from waters, he rescues others through waters. The man drawn out of the Nile will draw Israel out of Egypt through the Red Sea.

heb.paragraph: V.3: The word for the papyrus basket is tēbâ — the same word used for Noah's ark (Gen 6:14). The deliberate echo is theologically charged: as God preserved humanity through Noah's ark, so he preserves Israel's deliverer through Moses's basket. Both vessels float on waters of judgment; both carry the hope of a future beyond the flood.

The waterproofing materials Jochebed used on the basket echo Gen 6:14 where Noah waterproofs the ark with pitch (kōpher). This verbal connection reinforces the ark typology: this small vessel carries a salvation as cosmic in scope as Noah's, though hidden in the reeds of the Nile.

hist.historical: The Sargon birth legend (Akkadian, c.2300 BC) describes a baby placed in a basket of rushes, waterproofed with bitumen, set adrift on a river, and rescued by a royal figure — a striking parallel to Moses's birth narrative. This "exposed infant" motif was used across the ancient Near East to mark destined leaders. The parallels show Moses's story using conventional heroic-birth literary forms, while its theological distinctives (YHWH's purpose, the midwives, the mother's faith) remain unique.
hist.context: Pharaoh's decree was "throw every son into the Nile" (1:22). Jochebed uses the Nile to save her son — the instrument of Pharaoh's genocide becomes the vehicle of Israel's deliverer. Moses's very survival is an ironic reversal of Pharaoh's plan. His adoption into Pharaoh's household gives him the education and access that will later enable him to confront Pharaoh directly.

ane.parallel: Legend of Sargon of Akkad (c. 23rd cent. BCE)
ane.similarity: Sargon's birth legend states: 'My mother, a high priestess, conceived me in secret. She set me in a basket of rushes, sealed with bitumen, and cast me into the river. The river carried me to Akki, the water-drawer, who raised me.' Moses' mother places him in a basket of papyrus, coated with tar and pitch, among the reeds of the Nile.
ane.difference: Sargon's story is told to legitimize his rise to power — an unknown child becomes king. Moses' story explains why Israel's deliverer was raised as an Egyptian prince. The basket motif serves opposite social trajectories: Sargon rises from obscurity to royalty; Moses will leave royalty to identify with slaves.
ane.significance: The parallel is widely recognized but the dependency direction is debated. Both traditions use the exposed-child motif, but Genesis-Exodus subverts the ANE convention: God's chosen leader is identified not with royal power but with the oppressed.

refs.note: The basket (tevah) is the same word as Noah's ark. Both are instruments of preservation through water — both preserve the bearer of God's next covenant purpose.
"By faith Moses' parents hid him for three months after he was born, because they saw he was no ordinary child, and they were not afraid of the king's edict."
Stephen: "At this time Moses was born, and he was beautiful before God. And he was brought up for three months in his father's house... and Pharaoh's daughter adopted him."

sarna.source: Nahum Sarna, JPS Torah Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Sarna notes the birth narrative uses the minimum of names and details — the parents are not named here, the mother is identified only as a "daughter of Levi." The sparseness is not carelessness; it is narrative technique. The focus is entirely on the child. The extraordinary elements — the fine baby, the three months of concealment, the ark — are foregrounded. The parents' names (Amram and Jochebed) appear only in 6:20, a retrospective insertion. The birth narrative keeps Moses' story unencumbered by genealogy.

The tevah (basket/ark) is a hapax construction in this context — the term appears elsewhere only for Noah's vessel. Sarna argues the echo is intentional: both Noah and Moses are preserved through waters that bring death to others; both emerge as agents of a new beginning for God's people. The parallel is literary and theological, not incidental. Moses' survival is framed as cosmically significant as the survival of Noah.

The name "Moses" (Mosheh) is given by Pharaoh's daughter with the explanation "I drew him out of the water." Sarna notes that in Hebrew the name most naturally means "the one who draws out" (active), not "the one drawn out" (passive, which would be moshui). The princess names him with what he will do, not what was done to him. This is either the narrator's subtle wordplay on the name the princess gave, or a divine irony built into the naming — the name she chooses for the rescued child is the name of the rescuer he will become.

The birth narrative is stripped of almost all individuating detail — no names for the parents, no specification of the house, no description of the concealment. Alter notes the technique creates a kind of fairy-tale or type-scene quality: a beautiful child is born, hidden, endangered, discovered. The universality of the type-scene (the birth of the hero) means Moses' story plugs into a deep narrative archetype. But the specific details — the Levitical heritage, the Nile decree, the Egyptian princess — make it distinctively this story.

The scene at the river is choreographed with precision. The mother places the basket, the sister watches from a distance. Two women, two stations, two perspectives on the same child. Alter notes the narrative withholds any internal perspective on the mother's feelings — we are shown only the action. The restraint makes the scene more affecting, not less. The reader fills in what the text does not say.

The dialogue in vv.7-9 is the scene's narrative climax, told in rapid exchanges. Miriam's offer — "shall I go and call a Hebrew woman to nurse him?" — is delivered directly to the princess with confident brevity. The princess's response — "yes, go" — is equally terse. Jochebed is then summoned and the princess instructs her to nurse her own child. The irony of the scene is achieved without any editorial comment: the narrator presents it flatly and lets the reader see what the characters apparently do not.

Calvin on Jochebed seeing Moses was a "fine child": "The singular beauty of the child was a kind of presage of his future excellence." He reads the mother's perception as prophetic intuition — she perceives something extraordinary in this child before anyone else does. But he also emphasises the faith aspect: "She was not led away by blind maternal love, but by a divine instinct." Hebrews 11:23 will confirm this reading — it was faith, not sentiment, that drove her decision.

Calvin on Pharaoh's daughter: "God had destined her to perform a great office — to nourish his prophet and the future redeemer of his people, and thus to frustrate the counsel of the tyrant." Her compassion is providentially arranged: she happens to bathe at that place, at that time, and her compassion overrides her political loyalty. For Calvin, this is not chance but governance: "Nothing is more effectual for softening our hearts than the sight of distress."

The divine irony of Jochebed being paid to nurse her own son is not lost on Calvin: "The providence of God, in a wonderful manner, provided for the safety of the infant... God had even arranged that the boy should be nursed by his own mother, and maintained at the king's expense." The oppressor funds the education of his own downfall. Calvin sees this as characteristic of divine providence — working through the instruments of opposition to achieve its own ends.

The NET note on tevah (basket/ark) discusses the Egyptian loan-word dbt (a box or chest) as the possible etymology, though the Hebrew connection to Noah's ark is unmistakable. The note also explains "bulrushes" (gome) as papyrus reeds, widely used for basket-making in ancient Egypt. The bitumen and pitch (hemar and zefet) waterproofing is paralleled in Egyptian texts on papyrus boat construction. The basket was a real, practical object — waterproofed, buoyant, large enough for an infant.

The NET note on the name Moses discusses the Egyptian etymology (ms/mose, "son of" or "is born," as in Thut-mose, Ra-messes). The Hebrew popular etymology ("drawn out") is a wordplay on the Egyptian name that also encapsulates Moses' role. Ancient names frequently carried both an official etymology and a popular folk etymology that expressed the name's deeper significance. Both etymologies — Egyptian birth-name and Hebrew theological wordplay — are operating simultaneously in the text.

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

sections.header: Verses 11–22 — Moses Kills an Egyptian; Flees to Midian

poi.name: Egypt — Hebrew settlement area
poi.text: Moses goes out to where his own people are and sees their forced labor. His identification with the Hebrews is geographical — he goes to their working spaces, not just their domestic ones. The Egyptian he kills is struck down in the context of forced labor, not random violence.

poi.name: Midian
poi.coords: Northwestern Arabian Peninsula; east of Sinai
poi.text: Moses flees to Midian after the killing — eastward across the Sinai Peninsula. Midian is the territory of the descendants of Abraham and Keturah (Gen 25:2), making Moses's refuge in the extended covenant family. Jethro is a priest of Midian.

notes.note: MacArthur: Moses's intervention to protect the Hebrew slave is the first act of the man who will become Israel's deliverer. The motive is just; the method is wrong. Stephen notes (Acts 7:25) that Moses supposed his brothers would understand God was working through him, but they did not.

MacArthur: Who made you ruler and judge over us? -- the rejection of Moses by his own people foreshadows Israel's pattern of rejecting its deliverers and anticipates their rejection of the greater Moses (Acts 7:35).

heb.paragraph: The unnamed sister (v.4) who watches and intervenes is Miriam, named later (15:20). Her standing at a distance (mēraḥōq) — observant, ready, courageous — models the prophetic posture: watchfulness that enables timely action. Her offer to find a Hebrew nurse reunites the family God had almost scattered.

Moses's refusal of Egyptian wet-nurses (v.7, implied) leads to the child being nursed by his own mother — a providential irony: Pharaoh's daughter pays the child's mother to nurse her own son. Jochebed is able to form Moses's earliest religious identity before the Egyptian court absorbs him.

hist.historical: Midian was a tribal region in the northwestern Arabian Peninsula, east of the Gulf of Aqaba. The Midianites were descendants of Abraham through Keturah (Gen 25:2) — distant relatives of Israel. The identification of Jethro/Reuel as a "priest of Midian" has puzzled scholars: was he a pagan priest or a worshipper of YHWH? His later reception of Moses' God-story with worship (18:10-12) suggests pre-existing monotheistic inclinations. Moses' forty years in Midian were years of formation — shepherd, husband, father — preparing him for the wilderness years ahead.
hist.context: Moses's killing of the Egyptian (v.12) and his subsequent flight are not a mere biographical episode — they reveal the tension in his identity (Egyptian by upbringing, Hebrew by birth) and the fact that his first attempt at deliverance is done in his own strength and fails. The bush will come later, when God equips him. The rejection by his own people (v.14: "who made you a prince?") anticipates the pattern of prophetic rejection throughout Israel's history.

refs.note: Stephen quotes this episode directly: "When he was forty years old, it came into his heart to visit his brothers... he supposed that his brothers would understand that God was giving them salvation by his hand."
"By faith Moses... refused to be called the son of Pharaoh's daughter, choosing rather to be mistreated with the people of God than to enjoy the fleeting pleasures of sin."
Moses' two sons' names — Gershom ("sojourner there") and Eliezer ("my God is help") — together summarise Moses' Midian experience: alien and rescued.

sarna.source: Nahum Sarna, JPS Torah Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Sarna notes Moses' emergence from Pharaoh's palace to "his brethren" is the narrative hinge between his Egyptian formation and his Israelite identity. The word "brethren" (echav) appears three times in vv.11-13, each time marking a kinship claim that overrides his adopted status. Sarna compares this to the moment when Joseph identified himself to his brothers — the hidden identity disclosed. Moses makes his identification active, going out rather than waiting to be found.

The well-scene in Midian is the third type-scene in Genesis-Exodus involving a man meeting his future wife at a well (Gen 24; 29; Exod 2). Sarna identifies the standard elements: journey to foreign land, meeting at well, act of assistance, invitation to family home, eventual betrothal. Each instance of the type-scene carries variations; here Moses acts with force (driving away the shepherds) rather than the tender gestures of Jacob. His character is already the character of a leader who acts decisively on behalf of the vulnerable.

The triple use of "his brothers" / "his people" in vv.11-13 marks Moses' decisive identification. Alter notes the contrast: in Egypt he was the son of Pharaoh's daughter; here he identifies himself repeatedly as belonging to the Hebrews. The identification costs him everything — he will lose his palace, his status, his safety. The narrative does not pause to note this cost; it simply shows the act. The reader supplies the enormity of the choice.

"Moses was content to dwell with the man" — a remarkable statement after forty years in the Egyptian court. Contentment with simplicity, with the life of a shepherd, with a foreign wife and a name that means "foreigner": Moses' Midian years are years of deliberate reduction. Alter reads the contentment as preparation: the man who will lead a people through the wilderness must first live the wilderness, must first know what it is to be a ger, an outsider, dependent on strangers' hospitality.

Calvin on Moses' killing of the Egyptian: he does not excuse it but contextualises it — Moses acted "with more of human zeal than of the spirit of God." The impulse was right; the method was premature. Moses had not yet received the call, the authority, or the preparation. His act prefigures his role but does not constitute it. "God wishes his servants to attempt nothing except at his call and by his command." The forty years in Midian are the schooling that this precipitate act made necessary.

Moses' flight to Midian is providentially arranged. Calvin: "God, by his wonderful providence, so guided the course of Moses that he might be fitted by a long exercise of patience for the discharge of his office." The wilderness of Midian, the simplicity of shepherding, the foreignness and obscurity — all of these are divine pedagogy. Moses will lead Israel through wilderness; he must first be shaped by wilderness himself.

The NET note on "his brothers" (echav) discusses the significance of the term: Moses uses the kinship language of tribal belonging, not the language of political identity. He identifies with the slaves, not the court. The note connects this to Heb 11:24-25, where the author explicitly treats Moses' identification as a faith-act. The choice to "go out to his brothers" was a theological decision about identity and loyalty.

The NET note on Gershom explains the folk etymology: "I have been a sojourner (ger) in a foreign land (sham)." The name encapsulates Moses' dual alienation — he is a Hebrew in Egypt by origin, an Egyptian in training by upbringing, and a foreigner in Midian by flight. He belongs fully nowhere. This is the condition that will make him the ideal mediator: he has been an outsider in every world he has inhabited.

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

tl.name: God hears Israel's groaning — this passage
tl.text: After a long time (v.23) the king of Egypt died. At the end of 40 years in Midian, the groaning of Israel reaches God. The timing is not accident — 400 years of sojourn are nearing completion (Gen 15:13).

notes.note: MacArthur: God heard their groaning and he remembered his covenant with Abraham, with Isaac and with Jacob. God's action in deliverance is grounded in covenant faithfulness, not in response to Israel's goodness. They cried out; God remembered his covenant.

MacArthur: The three verbs summarise the covenant mechanism: God heard their groaning, God remembered his covenant, God looked on the Israelites. None of these imply God had forgotten; they signal the moment of covenant activation. The Exodus proceeds from God's remembering, not from Israel's achieving any threshold of worthiness.

heb.paragraph: V.25: "God saw the Israelites and was concerned about them." The verb rāʾā (see/look upon) here carries covenantal weight — not mere observation but responsive attention. This verse closes the chapter of preparation and opens the Exodus proper: the God who "sees" (cf. Hagar's El-Roi, Gen 16:13) has now turned his gaze toward his people's suffering.

V.17: Moses's first act of deliverance — protecting Jethro's daughters from aggressive shepherds — establishes his character as a mōshîaʿ (one who saves/delivers). This is the same root as "Yeshua" (Jesus). The deliverer of Israel is introduced through a micro-redemption in a foreign land, foreshadowing the macro-redemption to come.

hist.historical: The 'long period' of oppression fits either the late-date Exodus (c. 1250 BC, under Ramesses II and his successor Merneptah) or the early-date view (c. 1446 BC). The phrase 'the king of Egypt died' marks a political transition. The theological note that 'God heard their groaning and remembered his covenant' uses covenant-treaty language — Israel's cry activates the suzerainty obligations God assumed with Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob.
hist.context: Exodus 2:23-25 is a theological hinge paragraph — the transition from the human story to the divine response. Four verbs describe God's engagement: he heard their groaning, he remembered his covenant, he saw the people of Israel, he knew (v.25). The final verb — "God knew" — echoes the Pharaoh who "did not know" (1:8). The contrast is total: the one who chose not to know is answered by the One who fully knows. The stage is set for the burning bush.

refs.note: God told Abraham: "your offspring will be sojourners in a land that is not theirs... I will bring judgment on the nation that they serve." Exodus 2:24 is the moment God begins to act on this ancient promise.
"He has not hidden his face from him, but has heard, when he cried to him." The pattern of God hearing the cry of the afflicted is the foundation of the lament Psalms.
Zechariah's song: "to show the mercy promised to our fathers and to remember his holy covenant." The same category — God remembering covenant — applies to the Incarnation.

sarna.source: Nahum Sarna, JPS Torah Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Sarna identifies this paragraph as a structural transition unit — it closes the introductory section (chs.1-2) and opens the main narrative (ch.3 onward). The four verbs (heard, remembered, saw, knew) have been called a "divine soliloquy" — they record God's internal response before any external action. Sarna notes that "knew" (vayyeda) in v.25 is used absolutely, without an object in the Hebrew — "God knew" — an expression of complete divine awareness that does not require specification. He knows everything, including what is not said.

The shift from human action to divine perception is marked by the absence of dialogue. For the first time, we are inside God's perspective. Alter notes the four verbs are arranged in ascending intimacy: hearing (acoustic) → remembering (cognitive) → seeing (visual) → knowing (relational). The progression moves from external to internal, from response to involvement. "God knew" is the most compressed and the most profound — and by leaving it without an object, the narrator makes it the most open: God knew everything that needed to be known.

Calvin on God remembering the covenant: "God is said to remember his covenant when he shows in reality that he is not unmindful of it." The remembering is not recollection but action — God brings the covenant to bear as a present operative reality. Calvin makes the practical application: "Whenever we are afflicted, let us know that God will at length remember his covenant." The believer's confidence is not in circumstances but in the covenant God who keeps his word across generations.

The NET note on "remembered" (wayyizkor) explains the Hebrew idiom: God's remembering is always prospective and active — it means God is about to act in fulfilment of what he has committed to. The note cites Gen 8:1 (God remembered Noah) and Gen 30:22 (God remembered Rachel) as parallel uses: in each case, the "remembering" immediately precedes decisive divine action. The term is a narrative signal: when God remembers, rescue follows.

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET note on "remembered" (wayyizkor) explains the Hebrew idiom: God's remembering is always prospective and active — it means God is about to act in fulfilment of what he has committed to. The note cites Gen 8:1 (God remembered Noah) and Gen 30:22 (God remembered Rachel) as parallel uses: in each case, the "remembering" immediately precedes decisive divine action. The term is a narrative signal: when God remembers, rescue follows.

The NET note on four verbs of divine response — heard, remembered, looked, was concerned — identifies this as a covenant-attention formula. Each verb describes a different dimension of God's active engagement: he hears the audible cry, remembers the binding promise, looks at the visible situation, and cares about the personal need.

ppl.name: Moses↗ People
ppl.text: Moses stands at the centre of the Pentateuch’s narrative — the one man who spoke with God face to face (Exod 33:11; Num 12:8) and mediated the Sinai covenant to Israel. In Leviticus he receives the priestly legislation; in Numbers he leads, intercedes, and ultimately fails at Meribah (Num 20). The writer of Hebrews calls him "faithful in all God’s house" (Heb 3:5) while distinguishing him from Christ, the Son over the house.

ppl.name: Pharaoh
ppl.text: Context  Pharaoh's decree was "throw every son into the Nile" (1:22)…

ppl.name: Israel
ppl.text: Jochebed uses the Nile to save her son — the instrument of Pharaoh's genocide becomes the vehicle of Israel's deliverer…

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationESV"And God heard their groaning, and God remembered his covenant with Abraham, with Isaac, and with Jacob. God saw the people of Israel—and God knew."NIV"God heard their groaning and he remembered his covenant with Abraham, with Isaac and with Jacob. So God looked on the Israelites and was concerned about them."NLT"God heard their groaning, and he remembered his covenant promise to Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob. He looked down on the people of Israel and knew it was time to act."

src.title: Sargon Birth Legend (Akkadian, c.2300 BC)
src.quote: Describes a baby placed in a bitumen-sealed basket, set on a river, rescued by a royal figure. The "exposed infant" motif marks great leaders.
src.note: The Moses birth narrative uses a recognised ancient heroic-birth literary form while filling it with distinctive theological content.

src.title: Papyrus Anastasi VI
src.quote: Documents Shasu (Bedouin/Semitic) peoples from Edom/Sinai area entering Egypt — parallels Moses's Midianite sojourn and return.
src.note: Midian as a location for Moses's exile fits the known geography of Semitic movement in the region.

rec.text: Moses's choice to identify with the Hebrews rather than be Pharaoh's grandson is Hebrews 11's central act of faith — "he considered the reproach of Christ greater wealth than the treasures of Egypt."

Stephen's sermon reads Moses's first act of deliverance (killing the Egyptian) as a misunderstood first coming — the people "did not understand." The parallel to Christ's rejection by his own people is deliberate.

rows.label: Three Rescues: Moses Rescued Three Times Before He Rescues
rows.text: Jochebed hides, then floats; Miriam watches; Pharaoh's daughter draws out. Moses is rescued from the very river used to kill Hebrew boys. The instrument of Pharaoh's genocide becomes the instrument of the rescuer's rescue.

rows.label: The Burning Bush: Fire Without Consumption
rows.text: The fire burns in the thorn-bush but does not consume it. The divine presence in the midst of lowly, unconsumable creation is the Incarnation's first image -- the holy God dwelling without destroying.

hebtext.note: V.3: The word for the papyrus basket is tēbâ — the same word used for Noah's ark (Gen 6:14). The deliberate echo is theologically charged: as God preserved humanity through Noah's ark, so he preserves Israel's deliverer through Moses's basket. Both vessels float on waters of judgment; both carry the hope of a future beyond the flood.

The waterproofing materials Jochebed used on the basket echo Gen 6:14 where Noah waterproofs the ark with pitch (kōpher). This verbal connection reinforces the ark typology: this small vessel carries a salvation as cosmic in scope as Noah's, though hidden in the reeds of the Nile.

The unnamed sister (v.4) who watches and intervenes is Miriam, named later (15:20). Her standing at a distance (mēraḥōq) — observant, ready, courageous — models the prophetic posture: watchfulness that enables timely action. Her offer to find a Hebrew nurse reunites the family God had almost scattered.

Moses's refusal of Egyptian wet-nurses (v.7, implied) leads to the child being nursed by his own mother — a providential irony: Pharaoh's daughter pays the child's mother to nurse her own son. Jochebed is able to form Moses's earliest religious identity before the Egyptian court absorbs him.

V.25: "God saw the Israelites and was concerned about them." The verb rāʾā (see/look upon) here carries covenantal weight — not mere observation but responsive attention. This verse closes the chapter of preparation and opens the Exodus proper: the God who "sees" (cf. Hagar's El-Roi, Gen 16:13) has now turned his gaze toward his people's suffering.

V.17: Moses's first act of deliverance — protecting Jethro's daughters from aggressive shepherds — establishes his character as a mōshîaʿ (one who saves/delivers). This is the same root as "Yeshua" (Jesus). The deliverer of Israel is introduced through a micro-redemption in a foreign land, foreshadowing the macro-redemption to come.

thread.text: The basket (tevah) is the same word as Noah's ark. Both are instruments of preservation through water — both preserve the bearer of God's next covenant purpose.
"By faith Moses' parents hid him for three months after he was born, because they saw he was no ordinary child, and they were not afraid of the king's edict."
Stephen: "At this time Moses was born, and he was beautiful before God. And he was brought up for three months in his father's house... and Pharaoh's daughter adopted him."

Stephen quotes this episode directly: "When he was forty years old, it came into his heart to visit his brothers... he supposed that his brothers would understand that God was giving them salvation by his hand."
"By faith Moses... refused to be called the son of Pharaoh's daughter, choosing rather to be mistreated with the people of God than to enjoy the fleeting pleasures of sin."
Moses' two sons' names — Gershom ("sojourner there") and Eliezer ("my God is help") — together summarise Moses' Midian experience: alien and rescued.

tx.title: Moses' name — Egyptian or Hebrew etymology?
tx.content: MT gives a Hebrew folk etymology: 'I drew him out (mĕšîtihû) of the water.' The LXX translates literally. However, the name Moses (mōšeh) is almost certainly Egyptian (ms/mose, 'born of'), cognate with names like Thutmose and Rameses. No ancient version acknowledges the Egyptian etymology.
tx.note: The biblical text gives an Israelite perspective on an Egyptian name. All ancient versions preserve the Hebrew wordplay, which functions as theological reinterpretation rather than linguistic analysis.

debate.title: Was Moses' Killing of the Egyptian Morally Justified?

positions.name: Justified: Righteous Resistance
positions.argument: "He avenged him that was oppressed" — Stephen treats Moses' act as righteous deliverance. Philo calls it execution of a just sentence on an unjust aggressor. Moses acted on behalf of a weaker party against an oppressor. The killing was not murder but justice.

positions.name: Not Justified: Premature Self-Appointment
positions.argument: Moses had not received the divine commission. He acted "with more human zeal than divine authority." The subsequent flight and forty years of preparation show that God did not endorse the method. The impulse was right; the timing and authority were absent. Moses needed to be trained into proper leadership, not rewarded for vigilante action.

positions.name: Complex: Morally Ambiguous Act of a Developing Leader
positions.argument: The text neither commends nor condemns the act explicitly. Moses' moral development is the subject — he is a man of passion and justice who must learn patience and divine dependence. The ambiguity is the point: leaders are not born fully formed. Moses' story is the story of preparation, and this act is part of what needed to be trained.
